chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly provides a working link to the source code and experimental artifacts in Section VIII-A (footnote 1): 'https://github.com/relab/iniva-artifacts'. This satisfies the requirement for explicit public repository links.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: Section VIII-B describes the experimental setup using a local cluster of 25 physical machines connected via a 10 Gbps TOR switch. The evaluation involves real hardware deployment (physical testbed) rather than just simulation, measuring latency, throughput, and CPU usage on actual nodes.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: Section III (System Model) contains a dedicated formal definition of the fault model. It explicitly defines the adversary controlling fraction m <= f = 1/3, assumes a synchronous network with bound delta, and specifies that denial-of-service attacks are out of scope while focusing on targeted vote omission.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: Section VIII-C provides rigorous data-driven analysis of trade-offs. The authors quantify the performance overhead (e.g., ~33% lower throughput compared to HotStuff), CPU usage reduction (~48% less), and latency increases due to tree communication and 2ND-CHANCE messages, presenting these as specific metrics in Figures 3a and 3b.

major_weakness: The paper does not exhibit any major weaknesses as it achieved the maximum score (2) across all four evaluated criteria.
